source.title: RNA-aptamers-in-droplets (RAPID) high-throughput screening for secretory phenotypes

initial_request.research_problem: High-throughput screening of secretory phenotypes is limited to fluorescence-based detection, creating a bottleneck in the design-build-test cycle for non-fluorescent targets.
initial_request.target_object: Tyrosine production and recombinant protein secretion in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
initial_request.known_context: Library generation capacity outpaces high-throughput testing and screening. Droplet microfluidic screening is limited to fluorescence-based detection, excluding most non-fluorescent targets.
constraints: Must enable ultrahigh-throughput screening of millions of variants
Must allow live cell recovery for downstream analysis

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library generation capacity outpaces high-throughput testing and screening, creating a bottleneck in the design-build-test cycle. Droplet microfluidic screening is limited to fluorescence-based detection, excluding most non-fluorescent targets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence to enable general ultrahigh-throughput droplet screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RNA aptamers can transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence, enabling general ultrahigh-throughput droplet screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use RNA aptamers, specifically Spinach aptamers, to convert target concentration into fluorescence signal.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Using aptamers, we transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence, allowing ultrahigh-throughput screening of millions of variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Establish design criteria for RAPID screening based on this hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: library generation capacity outpaces the rate of high-throughput testing and screening
product concentration must be measurable via fluorescence, the only current modality with sufficient signal to robustly detect in kilohertz flowing droplets
Using aptamers, we transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence, allowing ultrahigh-throughput screening of millions of variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that RNA aptamers can transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What conditions must be met to apply RAPID screening?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RAPID screening requires single-cell cultivation in droplets, aptamer-based target measurement, aptamer stability in presence of cells, and microfluidic sorting with live cell recovery.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Establish design criteria for RAPID screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RAPID requires four design conditions: metabolically active single-cell droplets, aptamer-based target measurement in picoliter droplets, aptamer sensitivity and specificity during the multi-day production phase with cells and medium present, and efficient microfluidic operations that recover live cells for downstream cultivation and sequencing.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test aptamer performance in droplets, as initial tests revealed poor performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: To apply RAPID, several conditions must be met: (i) single cells must be cultivated in a metabolically active and productive state in picoliter droplets; (ii) RNA aptamers must allow target concentration measurements in picoliter droplets; (iii) the aptamer must remain sensitive and specific even in the presence of the producer cell and growth medium for the several day production phase; and (iv) the microfluidic system must perform all requisite operations efficiently and allow recovery of live cells for downstream cultivation and sequencing analysis of responsible proteins or pathways.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Design criteria established for RAPID screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Aptamers that performed well in bulk performed poorly in droplets, especially with cells.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Aptamer performance in droplets can be optimized.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In our first droplet tests, however, we found that aptamers performing well in bulk performed poorly in droplets, particularly when incubated with cells.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct directed evolution libraries for tyrosine over-production to test RAPID screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: In our first droplet tests, however, we found that aptamers performing well in bulk performed poorly in droplets, particularly when incubated with cells.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Aptamer performance in droplets requires optimization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to construct directed evolution libraries for tyrosine over-production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR on aro4 gene can generate libraries for directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct two libraries: one targeting regulatory region of wild-type aro4 and one whole-gene mutagenesis of K229L mutant.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Each gene was PCR amplified in an error-prone manner using GeneMorph II kit (Agilent) following the protocol for “medium” error-rate (4.5–9 errors per kbp). Two libraries were made for the evolution of aro4: one derived from the wild-type gene and one from the K229L mutant.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use RAPID screening to identify mutations in α-mating factor secretory leader fused to streptavidin.

evidence.quote_or_span: Each gene was PCR amplified in an error-prone manner using GeneMorph II kit (Agilent) following the protocol for “medium” error-rate (4.5–9 errors per kbp).

To both de novo identify new mutations and further improve upon the best-reported Aro4p K229L mutant, we established two directed evolution libraries that targeted: (1) specifically the regulatory region of the wild-type protein (residues 191–263) and (2) the entire protein sequence in the background of the K229L mutant.

Libraries were constructed through mutagenizing the proposed regulatory region of Aro4p (residues 191-263) a-c and through whole-gene mutagenesis of the best previously reported variant aro4-K229L d-f.
Two libraries were made for the evolution of $aro4$ : one derived from the wild-type gene and one from the K229L mutant, which has been shown to exhibit lower feedback inhibition $^{47}$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed evolution libraries constructed for tyrosine over-production.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can RAPID screening identify mutations that improve protein secretion?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RAPID screening can identify mutations in α-mating factor secretory leader that increase streptavidin secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct a library of α-mating factor secretory leader variants fused to streptavidin and screen using RAPID.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A library of α-mating factor secretory leader variants fused to streptavidin was constructed and screened using RAPID. Protein secretion from an individual clone was increased nearly threefold over wild-type production using this approach.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform microfluidic droplet sorting to enrich high producers.

evidence.quote_or_span: Protein secretion from an individual clone was increased nearly threefold over wild-type production using this approach

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RAPID screening identified improved streptavidin secretion.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to sort droplets based on fluorescence to enrich high producers.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Microfluidic droplet sorting using custom fluorimeter and dielectrophoresis can enrich high producers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sort droplets from the tyrosine and streptavidin libraries using a custom built fluorimeter and dielectrophoretic sorting, recovering the brightest 0.1% of droplets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: To detect and sort microfluidic droplets using a fluorescence signal, we made use of a custom built fluorimeter and microscope. LabVIEW software with a user set threshold for sorting controlled a field programmable gate array card to apply a voltage to a high voltage amplifier to dielectrophoretically move the droplet into the sorted outlet. We sorted the library after 5 days of incubation, recovering the brightest 0.1% of droplets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze sorted variants for statistical enrichment of improved tyrosine production.

evidence.quote_or_span: To detect and sort microfluidic droplets using a fluorescence signal, we made use of a custom built fluorimeter and microscope.

LabVIEW (National Instruments) software with a user set threshold for sorting controlled a field programmable gate array card to apply a voltage to a high voltage amplifier (Teck) to dielectrophoretically move the droplet into the sorted outlet
We then sorted the library after 5 days of incubation, recovering the brightest 0.1% of droplets.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are sorted variants statistically enriched for improved tyrosine production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sorted variants are enriched for improved tyrosine production over randomly selected variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare sorted variants to unsorted library using Mann–Whitney U-test.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The sorted variants were statistically enriched for improved production over randomly selected variants from the library (p<0.05, Mann–Whitney U-test; Figs. 3b, e).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify top performing tyrosine variant and its mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: The sorted variants were statistically enriched for improved production over randomly selected variants from the library (p<0.05, Mann–Whitney U-test; Figs. 3b, e).
The sorted variants were statistically enriched for improved production over randomly selected variants from the library (p<0.05, Mann–Whitney U-test; Figs.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the top performing tyrosine variant and its mutations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Top performing variant aro4-9 has three coding mutations and improves tyrosine production 27-fold.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence and characterize top variant aro4-9.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The mutant aro4-9 derived from wild-type ARO4 (library 1) was 27-fold improved over wild-type and has three coding mutations (H230Y, K252N, and V262I; Fig. 3c).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze which mutation primarily drives improvement.

evidence.quote_or_span: The mutant aro4-9 derived from wild-type ARO4 (library 1) was 27-fold improved over wild-type and has three coding mutations (H230Y, K252N, and V262I; Fig. 3c).
The mutant aro4-9 derived from wild-type ARO4 (library 1) was 27-fold improved over wild-type and has three coding mutations (H230Y, K252N, and V262I; Fig.
3D protein structure visualization with labeled helices (H230Y, V262I, K252N) and a small green ligand (no text or symbols beyond labels)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: H230Y mutation identified as key driver.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can further mutagenesis of aro4-K229L yield even higher improvement?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutant aro4-229-9 will show 28-fold improvement over wild type and 21% improvement over aro4-K229L.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The mutant aro4-229-9 derived from further mutagenesis of aro4-K229L (library 2) showed a 28-fold improvement over wild type and a 21% improvement relative to aro4-K229L, and has two additional coding mutations (T46A and T207I, Fig. 3f).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that improved production is not due to growth effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: The mutant aro4-229-9 derived from further mutagenesis of aro4-K229L (library 2) showed a 28-fold improvement over wild type and a 21% improvement relative to aro4-K229L, and has two additional coding mutations (T46A and T207I, Fig. 3f).

The mutant aro4-229-9 derived from further mutagenesis of aro4-K229L (library 2) showed a 28-fold improvement over wild type and a 21% improvement relative to aro4-K229L, and has two additional coding mutations (T46A and T207I, Fig.
3D protein structure visualization with labeled residues K229L, T46A, and T207I (no text or symbols beyond labels)
The mutant aro4-9 derived from wild-type ARO4 (library 1) was 27-fold improved over wild-type and has three coding mutations (H230Y, K252N, and V262I; Fig. 3c).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is improved production due to increased cell growth?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cell growth rate is not affected by improved enzyme mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure cell growth rate of improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Importantly, while each improved enzyme possesses novel mutations that increase tyrosine production, cell growth rate is not affected and, thus, these variants could not have been enriched for and discovered via growth selections (Supplementary Fig. 9).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate RAPID screening for streptavidin secretion enrichment.

evidence.quote_or_span: Importantly, while each improved enzyme possesses novel mutations that increase tyrosine production, cell growth rate is not affected and, thus, these variants could not have been enriched for and discovered via growth selections (Supplementary Fig. 9).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does RAPID screening enrich high streptavidin secretors?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RAPID screening enriches high streptavidin secretors, with some sorted variants showing significantly increased secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Re-transform sorted and unsorted variants and measure streptavidin titer.
evolution_trajectory.observation: After re-transforming individual randomly selected variants from the sorted and unsorted populations into a fresh yeast strain, we measured streptavidin titer in supernatant samples from shake-flask production cultures and found that two of the seven variants isolated by droplet sorting have significantly increased secretion (p < 0.01, One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Dunnett's Test for multiple comparisons to WT control).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude overall validation of RAPID approach.

evidence.quote_or_span: After re-transforming individual randomly selected variants from the sorted and unsorted populations into a fresh yeast strain, we measured streptavidin titer in supernatant samples from shake-flask production cultures and found that two of the seven variants isolated by droplet sorting have significantly increased secretion (p < 0.01, One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Dunnett's Test for multiple comparisons to WT control).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RAPID screening validated for both tyrosine and streptavidin.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does RAPID demonstrate generality across different secretory phenotypes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RAPID enhances tyrosine production up to 28-fold and recombinant protein secretion up to 3-fold in yeast.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Summarize overall validation of RAPID approach.
evolution_trajectory.observation: We demonstrate the RAPID approach by enhancing production of tyrosine and secretion of a recombinant protein in Saccharomyces cerevisiae by up to 28- and 3-fold, respectively.

evidence.quote_or_span: We demonstrate the RAPID approach by enhancing production of tyrosine and secretion of a recombinant protein in Saccharomyces cerevisiae by up to 28- and 3-fold, respectively.
Tyrosine production was increased nearly 28-fold over the wild-type production using this approach

validation_methods: Re-transformation of sorted and unsorted variants into fresh yeast strain to eliminate strain adaptations
Shake-flask production cultures for tyrosine and streptavidin quantification
Statistical analysis (Mann–Whitney U-test, ANOVA with Dunnett's Test)
metrics: Tyrosine production fold improvement over wild-type
Streptavidin secretion fold improvement over wild-type
success_verification.final_conclusion: RAPID enhanced tyrosine production up to 28-fold and recombinant protein secretion up to 3-fold in yeast, demonstrating generality.
limitations: Aptamers that performed well in bulk performed poorly in droplets, especially with cells, requiring optimization.
Cell growth rate not affected by improved enzyme mutations, so variants could not be discovered via growth selections.
